text: So the operation I'm going to show you is an operation I did at the end of twenty seventeen. The patient is a thirty three year old female who is five foot eight inches tall and weighs three hundred twenty six pounds. Her BMI is fifty. She lost twenty pounds in preparation for this operation.
Upon entering the abdomen, the surgeon retracts the liver to reveal the stomach, confirming the absence of a hiatal hernia.

This actually I think is the most important part of the operation and that is planning out exactly where I'm going to divide across the stomach while the stomach is still in its initial undisturbed form. As soon as I start manipulating the stomach by having the anesthesiologist pass a tube down to the stomach or by dividing the attachments along the side of the stomach or by pulling on the stomach to position it for the stapler, it causes distortion of the normal anatomy of the stomach because the stomach is very elastic, very stretchy. And if I staple across a distorted stomach, I get a distorted sleeve.
By planning out exactly where I'm going to divide across the stomach with the stomach in its initial undisturbed state, I can construct a very exact and a very consistent sleeve.

The surgeon uses a felt pen to draw lines on the stomach, marking surgical boundaries. A measuring tape is used to ensure precision, with a lower vertical line positioned 5 centimeters above the stomach's bottom and an upper horizontal line near the angularis. The marked area below the angularis is 4 centimeters wide, while above it, the width is set between 2.5 to 3 centimeters.

Using these dimensions of four centimeters wide below the angularis and two and a half centimeters to three centimeters wide above the angularis, I will end up with a small volume sleeve of three to four ounces in volume. In my experience, this is ideal. Any smaller and there can be too much restriction, any larger and weight loss isn't as good. this three to four ounce volume will expand to approximately six to eight ounces over time.
The surgeon divides the attachments along the greater curvature of the stomach, progressing towards the diaphragm to prepare for the gastric division.

The surgeon divides the attachments along the greater curve of the stomach, visible on the right side of the screen, freeing the back wall of the stomach. The lesser curve is visible on the left side.

The surgeon has completed freeing the entire greater curve of the stomach. The anesthesiologist inserts a 36 French bougie tube, measuring 12 millimeters in diameter, into the distal stomach to guide the gastric sleeve formation.

The surgeon applies a Cavidian stapler with a black cartridge to the marked line on the stomach, accommodating the thicker tissue at the bottom. The stapler is fired, ensuring the sleeve is precisely four centimeters wide at this location, as indicated by the movement of the marked line.
The surgeon observes the staple line along the stomach, noting its neat and precise appearance after the application of the stapler.

The surgeon applies the stapler with a black cartridge to the side of the gastric sleeve, maintaining equal distance from the lesser curve along both the back and front walls of the stomach. This is the second application of the stapler, using larger staples suitable for thicker stomach tissue.

The surgeon carefully aligns the stapler along the marked line on the stomach, ensuring precise placement to create a consistent sleeve. Pulling on the stomach visibly distorts the line, highlighting the importance of accurate alignment.

The surgeon's right hand holds the stapler, while the assistant's right hand is visible entering from the right side of the screen. The assistant's left hand operates the camera, and the surgeon's left hand is positioned in the upper left of the frame. The surgeon fires the stapler along the marked line on the stomach tissue.
The surgeon observes a well-formed staple line along the edge of the gastric sleeve, indicating precise alignment as intended.
The surgeon measures the upper part of the gastric sleeve above the level of the angularis, marked by a horizontal purple line, aiming for a width of two and a half to three centimeters.

The surgeon positions the initial staple firing at the bottom of the stomach, ensuring the width is four centimeters to achieve a sleeve with an overall volume of three to four ounces. The staple line is placed two to six centimeters from the bottom to control the final sleeve volume.

The surgeon uses a purple marking line to measure the width of the stomach along the greater curvature, ensuring it is between two and a half to three centimeters wide. The stomach is manipulated to avoid distortion before applying the stapler, aiming for a sleeve width under three centimeters for increased restriction.

The surgeon marks the upper part of the stomach with a bougie in place to identify the lesser curve, which is bordered by fat. The stapler is positioned along the purple line to create a sleeve approximately two and a half centimeters wide. The assistant holds the stomach to ensure precise alignment of the stapler on the purple line. The staple line appears straight and follows the lateral margin of the sleeve. The surgeon checks the width with the open jaw of the stapler before firing.
The surgeon performs an additional firing of the stapler to complete the division of the stomach, ensuring the staple line is properly formed along the edge of the sleeve.
The surgeon reinforces the top part of the staple line with sutures, visibly pulling the stomach over the staple line to reduce tension and lower the risk of leaks during healing.

The surgeon removes the gastrogrammat through the abdominal wall and performs a leak test by occluding the sleeve distally while the anesthesiologist injects air to distend the sleeve. The surgeon uses a tape measure to verify the dimensions of the sleeve, confirming the upper part is between 2.5 and 3 centimeters wide, with the angularis at 3 centimeters and the antrum at 4 centimeters wide. The staple line begins 4 centimeters before the pylorus. The final inspection shows the sutured portion is intact, with no bleeding or hiatal hernia, and the sleeve appears symmetric with a straight staple line.

If you'd like to see this operation uncut in its entirety, you can find it at the OLLIAC Center YouTube channel. If you'd like to learn more about our practice or read more about patient outcomes after weight loss surgery, you can visit us online at www.olliaccenter.com.
